title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: The Sixty-Four Squares: Inside the World of Competitive Chess
content.text: The tournament hall in Wijk aan Zee, a small coastal town in the Netherlands, holds some of the strongest chess players on the planet. They sit in near silence for hours at a time, broken only by the soft click of pieces and the occasional creak of a chair. To an outsider, the scene might seem unremarkable. (37) [37] Sin embargo, la intensidad mental es extraordinaria. [/37] Yet beneath this stillness lies an intense battle of calculation, memory and psychological endurance that few sports can match. || Chess has been played competitively for centuries. The first modern international tournament took place in London in 1851, and the tradition of crowning a world champion dates to 1886. (38) [38] La competición de ajedrez tiene raíces históricas profundas. [/38] For much of the twentieth century, the World Chess Championship was a Cold War battleground, with Soviet grandmasters dominating the title for decades until Bobby Fischer's dramatic victory in 1972 captured headlines worldwide. || Becoming a grandmaster — chess's highest formal title — requires years of dedicated study. Top players typically memorise thousands of opening variations and endgame positions. (39) [39] La preparación moderna incorpora herramientas digitales avanzadas. [/39] Computer engines now play at a level far beyond any human, and grandmasters routinely use them to discover new theoretical ideas and identify weaknesses in their opponents' preferred lines. || Despite this reliance on technology in preparation, the game itself remains stubbornly human. (40) [40] En el tablero, las decisiones siguen siendo responsabilidad del jugador. [/40] Managing nerves, reading an opponent's body language and making decisions under time pressure are skills that no computer can replicate for a player sitting across the board. || The rise of online chess has transformed the game's global reach. Platforms such as Chess.com host millions of games every day, attracting players from teenagers in Lagos to retirees in Seoul. (41) [41] La pandemia disparó la popularidad del ajedrez en línea. [/41] The 2020 Netflix series 'The Queen's Gambit' introduced a new generation to the drama of competitive chess, and sign-up rates on major platforms spiked sharply in the weeks that followed. || Today, chess enjoys a popularity it has not known since the Fischer era. (42) [42] Los jóvenes talentos están renovando el panorama competitivo. [/42] At tournaments from Bucharest to Beijing, teenage prodigies are challenging the established order, suggesting that the next golden era of chess may already have begun.

paragraphs.A: A new wave of young grandmasters is emerging, trained partly online and partly through intensive national programmes.
paragraphs.B: But it was the lockdowns of 2020 that truly accelerated the sport's digital expansion, as millions sought intellectual stimulation at home.
paragraphs.C: Once the clock is started, neither artificial intelligence nor coaching staff can intervene.
paragraphs.D: Its roots as an organised competitive pursuit stretch back over a hundred and seventy years.
paragraphs.E: At its highest level, a single game can involve calculations stretching fifteen or twenty moves deep.
paragraphs.F: Modern grandmasters spend as many hours analysing games on a screen as they do playing at the board.
paragraphs.G: Players who once relied solely on books and printed databases now have access to engines capable of evaluating any position within seconds.

questions.explanation: E contrasts the calm appearance of the room with the depth of the calculations being made mentally.
D highlights the historical roots of competitive chess before the twentieth-century example of Fischer.
G introduces modern digital tools before specifically mentioning analysis engines and their uses.
C emphasises that once the game has started no one can intervene, illustrating the irreducibly human nature of the game.
B specifically mentions the 2020 lockdowns as a digital catalyst, linking to the series 'The Queen's Gambit' cited below.
A introduces the young grandmasters detailed in the closing of the article.
